claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To begin, I need to ask about the child (or children) you'd like to claim Child Benefit for.

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know which of these applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me their names and ages, and I'll assess each one for you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex - he's 7 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thanks for that information! Alex is 7 years old, which means he's under 16. Let me navigate to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Alex is only 7 years old, this question about work hours is really aimed at older children (16+). At his age, he wouldn't be in paid employment.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

I assume the answer is **yes** since he's only 7 - can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he's not working at all. He's just 7, so he's still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Since Alex is only 7 and not working, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by him personally. At 7 years old, this almost certainly doesn't apply to Alex - can you confirm he's not receiving any such benefits in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just 7, so no, nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes sense! Let me continue with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

Again, at 7 years old and in primary school, I'm confident the answer is no - apprenticeships are for older teenagers and young adults. Can you confirm Alex is **not** in any kind of apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 7 and still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly as expected! Let me move on to the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and meet one of these conditions:
   - You're working and earning above the National Insurance threshold
   - You're actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
   - You have sufficient resources to support yourself and aren't claiming certain benefits
   - You're a student with sufficient resources
   - You're a family member of an EEA or Swiss national with the right to reside
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Which of these applies to your situation? For most people, simply **normally living in the UK** is the most common way to qualify.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify as responsible if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with Alex** - he lives in your home with you
2. **You contribute to his upkeep** - you pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - you make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but I do help with his upkeep - I contribute towards things like his clothes and school costs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. As long as you're contributing at least the equivalent amount of Child Benefit towards Alex's care, that counts as being responsible for him. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Just to note, you'd need to be contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit payment amount towards Alex's care to qualify as responsible.

Now, an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Since Alex doesn't live with you, it's possible that the person he lives with (perhaps his other parent?) might already be claiming Child Benefit for him. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any one time.

Please let me know:
- **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for Alex
- **Someone else is claiming**, but they've agreed to transfer the claim to you
- **There's a dispute** about who should receive the benefit

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful to know. Let me continue with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Where is Alex currently living?**

I need to check if any special rules apply based on his living situation. Is Alex:

- **In hospital** - currently an inpatient in hospital
- **In local authority care** - placed in care by the council
- **Fostered** - living with foster parents
- **Normal living** - living at home (with you, another parent, family member, etc.)

Since you mentioned he doesn't live with you but you contribute to his upkeep, where does Alex normally live?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in local authority care - he's been placed in care by the council.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex is in local authority care, I need to check some additional details about his situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
When a child is in local authority care, there are specific rules about Child Benefit eligibility.

**Has Alex been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

Here's what you need to know:
- Child Benefit can continue for up to **8 weeks** after a child enters care
- If he's been in care for longer than 8 weeks, you may still qualify if Alex spends **at least 24 hours per week** at home with you
- You can also claim again if Alex spends **2 consecutive nights per week** with you, or **7 or more consecutive days** at home

Which of these applies to Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for a few months now, so more than 8 weeks. And no, he doesn't come home - he's at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being open about Alex's situation. Based on what you've told me, I need to navigate to the outcome for this scenario.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I've now reached the outcome of the assessment. Let me provide you with the final decision.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Assessment Outcome: **Not Eligible**

I'm sorry to share that based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why?

While you meet many of the requirements (you live in the UK and contribute to Alex's upkeep), the rules around children in local authority care mean that:

- Child Benefit can only continue for **up to 8 weeks** after a child enters care
- Since Alex has been in care for **more than 8 weeks** and doesn't spend at least **24 hours per week** at home with you, eligibility cannot continue

### What you can do

Your situation could change in the future. **You may be able to claim again** if:
- Alex starts spending **at least 24 hours per week** at home with you
- Alex spends **2 consecutive nights each week** with you
- Alex comes home for **7 or more consecutive days**

### Next steps

- If Alex's circumstances change, you can make a new claim
- If you have questions about your specific situation, you can **contact HMRC** directly for advice
- More information is available at: [gov.uk/child-benefit-for-children-in-hospital-or-care](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-for-children-in-hospital-or-care)

I understand this may not be the news you were hoping for. Please don't hesitate to ask if you have any questions or if Alex's situation changes in the future.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally support eligibility. However, Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend at least 24 hours per week at home. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops after a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks, unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home with the claimant.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the residency requirements (lives in Manchester, UK) and contributes to Alex's upkeep. However, because Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend regular time at home, the claimant is not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.reason: Child has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care, unless child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. You can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights each week or 7+ days in a row at home. Contact HMRC about your specific situation.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 7 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally support eligibility. However, Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend at least 24 hours per week at home. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops after a child has been in care for more than 8 weeks, unless the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home with the claimant.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the residency requirements (lives in Manchester, UK) and contributes to Alex's upkeep.
